title: Cream tea scones
subtitle: Tall, tender, with a golden crust
excerpt: The classic British scone: tall and tender with a clean split to fill with clotted cream and jam. Cold butter, minimal handling, and a hot oven are the three things that matter.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: various scone and tea-bread recipes with rubbed-in fat and milk liquid. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): cream tea and afternoon tea scones as a regional British staple. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: plain raised cakes and tea scones with similar proportions. The cream tea serving convention (scone with clotted cream and jam) is associated with the West Country, particularly Devon and Cornwall, where rivalry over the order of application (cream first or jam first) has been documented since the early 20th century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze fully baked and cooled scones for up to 2 months, wrapped individually. Warm from frozen in a 160°C fan oven for 8–10 minutes. Unbaked cut rounds also freeze well; bake from frozen at 220°C fan adding 3–4 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: The recipe doubles well. Mix in two rounds if your bowl is small.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cut rounds can be refrigerated on the lined tray, covered, for up to 2 hours before baking.

recipeTools.notes: A 6 cm plain or fluted round cutter.

glossaryTerms.definition: Incorporating cold fat into flour by breaking pieces into the flour and rubbing them between your fingertips, lifting as you work to keep the mixture cool. The aim is a rough breadcrumb texture with some small butter pieces remaining. Warmth from over-handling melts the fat into the flour, which tightens gluten and makes the baked result tough and dense rather than short and tender.

content.text: A good cream tea scone has a clean horizontal split, a golden top, and a crumb that is short and tender without being cakey. Three things determine the result: cold butter rubbed lightly into the flour, a dough that is barely brought together, and a hot oven that drives a fast rise before the outside sets. Work quickly and without excess pressure at every stage.

This recipe uses self-raising flour with a teaspoon of extra baking powder for additional height. The liquid is buttermilk, which adds a gentle acidity that reacts with the raising agent and contributes to the characteristic flavour. Whole milk works if buttermilk is unavailable.
What you need

items.prepNote: or whole milk with 1 tsp lemon juice, left 5 minutes

content.text: Keep the butter in the fridge until the moment you need it. Cold fat held in small pieces gives the scone its rise; when those pieces melt in the oven, they create steam and small pockets of air that push the scone upward.
Method
Prepare the dry ingredients

Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Weigh {{self-raising-flour}} self-raising flour, {{baking-powder}} baking powder, and the salt into a large bowl and whisk briefly to combine.
Rub in the butter

Add {{unsalted-butter}} cold butter cubes to the flour. Working quickly, use your fingertips to break each cube into the flour and rub until the mixture resembles coarse, slightly uneven breadcrumbs with a few pea-sized pieces still visible. This is the
rubbing-in
stage. Lift the mixture as you work to aerate it. Stop before the mixture looks uniformly fine; residual butter pieces are correct and intentional.
Stir in {{caster-sugar}} caster sugar with a fork.
Add the liquid

Beat the egg into the {{buttermilk}} buttermilk in a jug. Make a well in the centre of the flour mixture and pour in most of the liquid, holding back about 2 tablespoons. Stir with a round-bladed knife using quick, light cutting strokes until the dough just comes together. Add the reserved liquid if needed to bring in any dry patches. The dough should be soft and slightly sticky but not wet.
Shape and cut

Tip the dough onto a lightly floured surface. Pat it out gently to about 2.5 cm thick — do not roll it thin and fold repeatedly, as this develops the gluten and toughens the crumb. Use a 6 cm round cutter dipped in flour to stamp out rounds, pressing straight down without twisting. Twisting seals the edge and prevents the scone from rising straight. Place the rounds on the lined tray.
Press the scraps together gently and pat out again for a second cut. The second-cut scones will be slightly less uniform but still good.
Glaze and bake

Brush the top of each scone with beaten egg. Avoid letting the egg run down the sides; an egg-sealed edge resists rising cleanly. Bake in the preheated oven for 11–13 minutes. The scones are done when the tops are deep golden-brown, the sides are set and dry rather than pale and doughy, and a scone tapped on the base sounds hollow. The colour on the top should be rich, not pale gold.
Cool and serve

Transfer to a cooling rack. Scones are best eaten within 2 hours of baking. Serve split across the natural horizontal line that forms during baking, with clotted cream and jam. Which goes on first is a matter of geography and strong opinion.
Troubleshooting

attrs.heading: When things go sideways
attrs.intro: Three problems that come up most often with scones.

items.symptom: Scones spread outward instead of rising upward
items.cause: Dough too wet, butter too soft, or cutter twisted during stamping.
items.fix: Keep butter cold. If the dough feels very sticky, add a little more flour. Press the cutter straight down without any rotation; a twist seals the cut edge and prevents a clean horizontal rise.

items.symptom: Scones are dense and heavy with a tight crumb
items.cause: Dough overworked after adding the liquid, or butter was rubbed in too thoroughly leaving no butter pieces.
items.fix: Mix liquid into flour with the minimum number of strokes needed to bring the dough together. Some unevenness is correct. Stop rubbing in while you can still see a few small butter pieces in the mixture.

items.symptom: Pale tops that look baked on the outside but doughy inside
items.cause: Oven not hot enough, or scones too thick.
items.fix: Scones need a very hot oven to rise before the crust sets. Verify temperature with an oven thermometer. Pat the dough to exactly 2.5 cm thick; thicker scones take longer and the crust sets before the centre cooks through.

content.text: The cream tea tradition

Scone recipes in British household cookbooks appear consistently from the mid-19th century onward, using rubbed-in fat, a raising agent, and milk or buttermilk. The cream tea serving convention is associated with the West Country. Devon and Cornwall both claim the tradition, and both dispute the other's order of application for the cream and jam. Neither side is likely to concede.
